tool_args.label: Life in the Slow Lane Report Card

payload.project_name: Life in the Slow Lane: A Report Card for New York City's Bus System
payload.description: A report card assessing every bus line in NYC on speed, bunching rate, and on-time performance using real-time MTA data from June 2024 to June 2025, assigning letter grades A through F.

evidence_refs.source_quote: Life in the Slow Lane: A Report Card for New York City's Bus System
This report card builds on that analysis, using real-time data from the MTA to provide a more detailed look into bus performance
This report card used live data from MTA's Bus Time feed collected between June 17, 2024 and June 12, 2025
People Oriented Cities. (2025). How Much Faster Are We Moving?

payload.description: Congestion pricing implemented in January 2025 in the Central Business District (CBD) of New York City.

evidence_refs.source_quote: Data collected from the MTA before and after the implementation of congestion pricing in January 2025

payload.description: 2018 NYC Department of Transportation plan that established a performance target of increasing bus speeds by 25% citywide—from approximately 8 to 10 miles per hour. Target has since been abandoned.

evidence_refs.source_quote: NYC Department of Transportation's Better Buses plan established a performance target of increasing bus speeds by 25% citywide—from approximately 8 to 10 miles per hour. That target has since been abandoned

payload.caption: Table 1: Bus Performance Metrics
rows: A bus's typical operating speed, weighted by how often it runs and controlled for borough and service type (i.e. express or local). A bus's average speed reflects dwell time, or the amount of time a bus spends at stops waiting for passengers to board.
Bunching Rate (for buses with headways of < 10 minutes)

Two or more buses are 'bunched' when observed headways are one-quarter or less of the scheduled headway. If buses on a route are scheduled to arrive every eight minutes, a bus two minutes or less behind the one preceding it is considered bunched. The grading methodology for this report used the inverse ('not-bunched rate'), where a higher value indicated better performance.
On-Time Rate (for buses with headways > 10 minutes)

A bus is 'on-time' if, at the point of observation, it arrived at a given stop within five minutes of its scheduled arrival time. A bus with an on-time rate of 65% shows up at its scheduled stop within five minutes of the scheduled time two-thirds of the time.

evidence_refs.source_quote: Table 1 summarizes the three key metrics this report card uses to assign a letter grade to every bus line in the city.

tool_args.label: Table 4: Congestion Pricing Impacts on Bus Performance

payload.table_title: Congestion Pricing Impacts on Bus Performance

evidence_refs.source_quote: letter grades were assigned using the following thresholds

tool_args.label: Majority of bus lines performing below average

payload.claim_text: 56% of bus lines (186 out of 332) received a grade of D or lower, meaning that they run at speeds significantly below the borough-wide average, are more likely to experience bunching, and consistently fail to arrive at scheduled stops on-time.
payload.statement: Majority of bus lines performing below average

evidence_refs.source_quote: 56% of bus lines (186 out of 332) received a grade of D or lower

tool_args.label: 73% of Manhattan buses grades D or F

payload.claim_text: Almost three-quarters (73%) of Manhattan buses have grades of D or F, worse than in any of the other four boroughs. This is due largely to heavy traffic in Manhattan, where some buses run at speeds as low as 5 mph.

evidence_refs.source_quote: Almost three-quarters (73%) of Manhattan buses have grades of D or F

payload.claim_text: 15% of Brooklyn buses are bunched, a rate considerably higher than the citywide average of 10.6%. This means that 15% of buses in Brooklyn fail to maintain even spacing along their routes, creating unreliable service and wait times regularly in excess of 10 minutes for riders on lines where average wait times should be no more than five minutes.

evidence_refs.source_quote: 15% of Brooklyn buses are bunched, a rate considerably higher than the citywide average of 10.6%

payload.claim_text: Express buses have higher average speeds but a much lower on-time reliability rate than the system overall. Many express buses travel across bridges, highways, and tunnels, allowing them to reach speeds most local buses cannot. However, they fail to reach stops at their scheduled times compared to local or SBS buses. All of the ten bus lines with on-time performance rates below 50% are express buses.

evidence_refs.source_quote: All of the ten bus lines with on-time performance rates below 50% are express buses.

payload.claim_text: SBS lines perform slightly better than local and express buses overall. 16% of SBS bus routes received A or B grades, compared to just 8% of routes overall. SBS buses also have higher average speeds and on-time performance rates, as well as lower bunching rates, than local routes. This is likely attributable to stop consolidation and all-door boarding on these routes.

evidence_refs.source_quote: 16% of SBS bus routes received A or B grades, compared to just 8% of routes overall

payload.claim_text: Between January 1 and June 1, 2025, reliability scores for buses operating in the CBD improved by 9.2% compared to the five months preceding implementation. Express buses account for much of this effect.

evidence_refs.source_quote: reliability scores for buses operating in the CBD improved by 9.2% compared to the five months preceding implementation
Citywide Average Speed (mph) 9.3

payload.claim_text: Potential performance targets could include: Increasing speeds by 15% citywide, moving 90% of C-grade bus lines to B-grade or better; Reducing the average bunching rate on high-frequency bus routes from 10.6% to 5%; and Improving reliability on the 40 bus lines performing worst on bunching and on-time performance metrics.

evidence_refs.source_quote: Increasing speeds by 15% citywide, moving 90% of C-grade bus lines to B-grade or better; Reducing the average bunching rate on high-frequency bus routes from 10.6% to 5%; and Improving reliability on the 40 bus lines performing worst on bunching and on-time performance metrics.

payload.claim_text: A weighted average of the two Z-scores was then computed for each bus line: 33% weight given to speed, and 66% weight given to reliability. This average z-score was then normalized and rescaled to a 4.0 academic GPA scale. Reliability accounts for two-thirds of a bus line's final grade.

evidence_refs.source_quote: 33% weight given to speed, and 66% weight given to reliability
A combination of the two metrics ('reliability') accounts for two-thirds of a bus line's final grade.
